coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor for determination of Golgi Protein 73 based on boron, nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots and copper metal-organic framework

coredata.dc:description: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC), characterized by poor early diagnosis, exhibits the second-highest lethality rate among malignancies. This clinical challenge underscores the significance of Golgi protein 73 (GP73) as a promising serum biomarker for HCC detection. Herein, a ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor was constructed employing a dual-signal modulation strategy. The system integrated boron, nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (BNCQDs) as the first fluorescent signal (I445) with a functional copper-based metalorganic framework conjugated with GP73-specific aptamer (Cu-MOF-Apt). The latter served dual functions: target recognition and peroxidase-mimetic catalyst for converting o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP), another fluorescent signal (I560). The peroxidase-like activity of Cu-MOF increased with the increase of GP73Apt attached. In the presence of GP73, target binding induced structural disintegration of Cu-MOF-Apt through GP73-Apt complex formation, thereby suppressing DAP generation. This target-responsive process led to the reduction of the fluorescence intensity of DAP and the increase of the fluorescence intensity of BNCQDs. Under the optimal conditions, the established ratiometric relationship (I445/I560 = 0.0007X + 0.7021, R2 = 0.997) enables quantitative detection of GP73 in the range of 25.00–600.00 ng/mL with the limit of detection (LOD) of 14.06 ng/mL. Clinical validation using serum specimens demonstrated excellent reproducibility (RSD 0.28 %–0.98 %) and recovery rates (99.75 %–107.49 %). The ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor's linear range effectively covers clinically relevant GP73 concentrations in HCC patients, while the robust serum analysis performance confirms its potential for practical diagnostic applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC), characterized by poor early diagnosis, exhibits the second-highest lethality rate among malignancies. This clinical challenge underscores the significance of Golgi protein 73 (GP73) as a promising serum biomarker for HCC detection. Herein, a ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor was constructed employing a dual-signal modulation strategy. The system integrated boron, nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (BNCQDs) as the first fluorescent signal (I445) with a functional copper-based metalorganic framework conjugated with GP73-specific aptamer (Cu-MOF-Apt). The latter served dual functions: target recognition and peroxidase-mimetic catalyst for converting o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP), another fluorescent signal (I560). The peroxidase-like activity of Cu-MOF increased with the increase of GP73Apt attached. In the presence of GP73, target binding induced structural disintegration of Cu-MOF-Apt through GP73-Apt complex formation, thereby suppressing DAP generation. This target-responsive process led to the reduction of the fluorescence intensity of DAP and the increase of the fluorescence intensity of BNCQDs. Under the optimal conditions, the established ratiometric relationship (I445/I560 = 0.0007X + 0.7021, R2 = 0.997) enables quantitative detection of GP73 in the range of 25.00–600.00 ng/mL with the limit of detection (LOD) of 14.06 ng/mL. Clinical validation using serum specimens demonstrated excellent reproducibility (RSD 0.28 %–0.98 %) and recovery rates (99.75 %–107.49 %). The ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor's linear range effectively covers clinically relevant GP73 concentrations in HCC patients, while the robust serum analysis performance confirms its potential for practical diagnostic applications.

Schematic principal diagram of BNCQDs/Cu-MOF-Apt/DAP ratio fluorescent aptasensor.
Image 1

ce:keyword.ce:text: Boron, nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) ranks as the second leading cause of cancer deaths, and one of the major reasons for its steadily increasing mortality rate year after year is poor early diagnosis [1]. With early intervention, the 5-year survival rate of HCC patients can exceed 86.20 %, but its complex pathologic features and individual heterogeneity pose a great challenge for early detection [2]. Existing reports have shown that the high expression of Golgi protein 73 (GP73) is positively correlated with the degree of tumor differentiation in HCC [3]. Zhang et al. obtained the conclusion that GP73 has a high diagnostic value for HCC after a meta-analysis of 8314 samples [4]. Therefore, the development of a faster and more accurate GP73 assay would be of great value in reducing the prevalence and lethality of HCC, as well as for human health. At present, there is no official reference range for serum GP73 levels in healthy and diseased populations, but Chen et al. [5] analyzed the adjuvant diagnostic and therapeutic value of GP73 that the potential risk of liver disease diagnosis increased by about 12-fold when the GP73 level was 135 ng/mL, which can be used as a reference range for serum GP73 levels in human beings.

Currently, the main detection methods for GP73 are the enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), as well as the chemiluminescence immunoassay and electrochemical method [6,7]. While these techniques have achieved good GP73 quantification, they suffer from inherent limitations including insufficient specificity and cumbersome operational procedures. In contrast, fluorescence-based analytical platforms have garnered significant attention due to their exceptional sensitivity, operational simplicity, and molecular discrimination capability [8]. For example, Liu et al. [9] engineered a Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) system employing CdTe/CdS quantum dots as fluorophores, GP73 antibodies for target recognition, and carboxyl-functionalized magnetic nanoparticles as fluorescence quenchers, achieving specific detection of GP73 through fluorescence recovery upon target binding. Yue et al. [10] developed a time-resolved fluorescence immunoassay (TRFIA) leveraging fluorescent lifetime variations in sandwich-type immunocomplexes, enabling reliable GP73 detection in both buffer solutions and clinical serum samples. These advancements highlight the pivotal role of fluorophore selection, as the photophysical properties of fluorescent probes fundamentally determine the analytical performance of fluorescence-based detection systems.

Within the spectrum of fluorescent nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) demonstrate exceptional chemical stability, biocompatibility, and rapid response kinetics [11,12], thereby CQDs have shown great potential for biosensing applications [13,14]. In addition, CQDs have good biological safety. For example, Wang et al. reported a sulfhydryl succinic acid-carbon quantum dots (MA-CQD) nanoenzyme, which can induce cell apoptosis and be used for tumor-specific therapy [15]. However, the low optical properties of CQDs hinder their further application [16]. Heteroatom doping serves as an effective electronic structure engineering strategy to address these deficiencies [17]. Among the doped elements, boron and nitrogen are common doping elements. Nitrogen's high electronegativity facilitates electron donation to modulate the π-conjugated system for CQDs to regulate their internal electronic environment. While boron's vacant p-orbitals generate electron-deficient domains that enhance radiative recombination efficiency through localized electron distribution [18,19]. The boron, nitrogen co-dopped CQDs (BNCQDs) exhibit ultra-stable photoluminescence with concentration-dependent emission wavelength modulation, overcoming conventional CQDs' optical limitations [20].

Mostly, conventional single-signal fluorescence detection systems are inherently susceptible to environmental interference and operator-dependent variability, often compromising measurement reproducibility and practical deployment. Compared with monochromatic fluorescence, ratiometric fluorescence sensing employs dual-emission signal self-calibration to effectively compensate for background noise and instrumental fluctuations, thereby achieving superior environmental robustness [21]. Two distinguishable signals with the proper response intensity are necessary for ratiometric fluorescence sensors [22,23]. For example, Shi et al. [24] investigated a ratio fluorescent probe (CQDs@ZIF-8@Apt-AuNPs) for the detection of abscisic acid (ABA) in rice seeds by utilizing the fluorescence properties of CQDs and AuNPs, with a linear detection range of 0.10–150 ng/mL and a minimum detection limit of 30.00 ng/mL. The above reports combined two fluorescent molecules with two fluorescent signals, which would complicate the manufacturing process. The development of single-component ratiometric sensors has been performed for this purpose. Liu et al. [25] cleverly utilized the fluorescent and peroxidase-like capability of aptamer-modified C3N4 nanosheets (Apt/C3N4 NSs), which had intrinsic blue fluorescence (445 nm) from quantum confinement effects, and peroxidase-mimetic activity enabling catalytic conversion of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to orange-emitting 2,3-diaminophenazine (560 nm). This self-referencing system achieved exosome detection through continuous fluorescence ratio modulation, circumventing multi-component assembly complexities.

Copper-based metal-organic frameworks (Cu-MOF) have good peroxidase-like activity due to the intrinsic properties of Cu2+ redox centers and the high surface area/porosity of MOF architectures. Cu-MOF can scavenge different types of reactive oxygen species such as hydroxyl radicals (·OH) for osteoarthritis and acute pancreatitis treatment [26,27]. Notably, their enzyme-like properties have been ingeniously harnessed in biosensing development [28,29] and can be utilized as an HRP enzyme in ratio fluorescent aptamer sensors [30]. In addition, Cu-MOF can absorb single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) through π-π stacking interactions and form covalent conjugates with amino-modified aptamers [31]. Meanwhile, the peroxidase-like activity of Cu-MOF changes with the degree of attachment of ssDNA on its surface which can be utilized to achieve specific detection of targets by aptamers [32]. Specifically, aptamer-functionalized Cu-MOF (Cu-MOF-Apt) demonstrate target-responsive catalytic enhancement, which can be used as a nanozyme for the oxidation of OPD to fluorescent molecules (DAP), another fluorescence signal for subsequent ratiometric fluorescence detection.

Herein, a BNCQDs/Cu-MOF-Apt/DAP ratiometric fluorescence aptasensor was constructed using BNCQDs as the first fluorescent signal (I445), aptamer-functionalized Cu-MOF (Cu-MOF-Apt) nanozymes with tunable peroxidase-like activity can enhance the efficiency of catalyzing the oxidation of OPD to fluorescent molecule (DAP) which served dual roles as secondary fluorophore (I560) and BNCQDs quencher via inner filter effect (IFE). The ratio of the two fluorescence signals (I445/I560) was used as the dependent variable of the aptasensor, which could improve the sensitivity of the aptasensor while avoiding environmental errors. In the presence of target GP73, the strong affinity between Apt and GP73 leads to the disintegration of Cu-MOF-Apt, resulting in a decrease of catalytic activity, thereby reducing the production of DAP and changing in the ratiometric fluorescence signal. The linear relationship between I445/I560 and the concentration of GP73 was obtained by detecting the changes in the fluorescence spectrograms after the addition of different concentrations of GP73. Herein, DAP acts as a source of absorber for BNCQDs fluorescence, and its signal is a benchmark for subsequent normalization of fluorescence intensity. The ratiometric fluorescence signal, based on an internal filtering effect (IFE) effect between DAP and BNCQDs, is dependent on the concentration of generated DAP, thus achieving highly facile and robust detection of GP73. This self-calibrating mechanism effectively compensates for environmental variations, achieving ultra-sensitive quantification of biomolecules.

The main chemical reagents used in this study and their sources are as follows. Terephthalic acid (TA, C8H6O4, 99.0 %) was from Shanghai rhawn Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. Glacial acetic acid (CH3COOH, AR), Citric acid (C6H8O7·H2O, 99.5 %), Boric acid (H3BO3, ≥99.5 %), ethylenediamine monohydrate (C2H8N2·H2O, 98.0 %), Copper nitrate trihydrate (Cu (NO3)2·3H2O, AR), O-phenylenediamine (OPD, 98.0 %), Sodium acetate (C2H3NaO2·3H2O, 99.0 %), Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30.0 % in H2O), Bovine Serum Albumin (BSA, grade of Molecular biology), Human Serum Albumin (HSA, 96.0 %), 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 98.0 %), N-Hydroxy succinimide (NHS, 98.0 %), and sucrose were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Alpha-fetoprotein (AFP, >85.0 %) and Immunoglobulin G (IgG, 85.0 %) were purchased from Beijing Sobaolai Technology Co., Ltd. Golgi protein 73 (GP73, >90.0 %), Glypican-3 (GPC3, ≥95.0 %), and Immunoglobulin E (IgE, >95.0 %) were provided by Shanghai Tide Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The aminated GP73 aptamer (GP73Apt, HPLC) with the following sequences (5′–NH2–C6-GCAGTTGATCCTTTGGATACCCTGG-3′) [33] was synthesized and puriffed by Shanghai Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

All absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra were obtained by UV-6100 (Metash, China) and F-320 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Guangdong, China). The scanning electron microscope (SEM) was measured by Regulus 8220 (Hitachi, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was obtained from JEM-1200EX (JEOL, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 100 kV and a TEM point resolution of 1.60 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was detected by Escalab 250 xi (Thermofisher, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) was measured by D8 Advance (Bruker, Germany) with a tube current of 40 mA, a tube voltage of 24 kV, and a wavelength (copper target) of 1.5406 Å. Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR) was obtained from Nicolet iS 10 (Thermofisher, USA). The characterization of electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) comes from the Burker A300 (Bruker, Germany).

BNCQDs were synthesized using the hydrothermal method [34]. First, citric acid (2.50 g) and boric acid (1.00 g) were dissolved in 15 mL of ultrapure water, and 1 mL of ethylenediamine (monohydrate) were gradually added as a nitrogen source during stirring. Second, the above solution was poured into a polytetrafluoroethylene-lined autoclave and reacted at 200 °C for 8 h. Third, after centrifugation (10000 rpm, 10 min) to remove large particles, the remained solution was put into the dialysis bags (500 Da) and dialyzed at 4 °C for 48 h to remove incompletely reacted raw materials. Fourth, after dialysis, the solution was further purified through a 0.22 μm microporous filter membrane to obtain the purified BNCQDs solution. Finally, the purified BNCQDs solution was transferred to a vacuum drying oven at 50 °C overnight to obtain BNCQDs powder. The quantum yield of BNCQDs was 8.67 %.

Cu-MOF was synthesized using a typical solvothermal method [32]. First, copper nitrate trihydrate (0.485 g) and terephthalic acid (TA, 0.335 g) were used as precursors and dissolved in 10 mL of N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF). Second, the above solution was stirred well, put into a PTFE-lined autoclave, and reacted at 150 °C for 3 h. Third, the reacted solution was centrifuged (10000 rpm, 9 min) to obtain the blue powder, which was washed three times with ultrapure water and anhydrous ethanol alternately, then dried in a vacuum oven at 50 °C overnight. Finally, the blue powder (0.1 mg) was dispersed in 1.0 mL deionized water to obtain 0.1 mg/mL Cu-MOF solution.

The Cu-MOF-Apt probe was prepared by combining the Cu-MOF with aminated GP73 aptamer (Apt) through covalent conjugation. Firstly, EDC/NHS (60 mM/30 mM) was added to 0.1 mg/mL of Cu-MOF solution at a volume ratio of 10:3 to activate the carboxyl groups for 30 min. Secondly, the above solution was mixed with 8.00 μM of GP73Apt at a volume ratio of 13:10 and incubated in a thermostatic shaking chamber at 35 °C for 12 h. After incubation, the resulting solution was obtained as the Cu-MOF-Apt probe, which was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for later use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroxidase-like properties of Cu-MOF and Cu-MOF-Apt
ce:section.ce:para: In the presence of H2O2, the catalytic efffciency of Cu-MOF and Cu-MOF-Apt, akin to peroxidase, was determined by their ability to catalyze the oxidation of OPD. For a typical reaction system, First, Cu-MOF and Cu-MOF-Apt solutions of the same concentration were prepared, i.e., 20 μL of 1.00 mg/mL Cu-MOF solution was divided into two parts, one part was added to 10 μL of pure water, and the other part was added to 10 μL of 8.00 μM GP73Apt and construct the Cu-MOF-Apt. Second, OPD was utilized as a chromogenic agent to explore the peroxidase-like activity of Cu-MOF by preparing the following five sets of solutions, respectively. Solution a: 20 μL OPD +20 μL H2O2 + 160 μL Buffer. Solution b: 20 μL OPD +20 μL Cu-MOF +160 μL Buffer. Solution c: 20 μL H2O2 + 20 μL Cu-MOF +160 μL Buffer. Solution d: 20 μL OPD +20 μL H2O2 + 20 μL Cu-MOF +140 μL Buffer. Solution e: 20 μL OPD +20 μL H2O2 + 20 μL Cu-MOF-Apt +140 μL Buffer.

The concentration of OPD was 100.00 mM, the concentration of H2O2 was 200.00 mM, and 200.00 mM of HAc-NaAc solution (pH 4.5) was used for the Buffer. After allowing the reaction to proceed for 1 h at room temperature, absorbance measurements at 420 nm were recorded utilizing a UV–vis spectrophotometer. In addition, the excellent affinity of Cu-MOF and Cu-MOF-Apt for OPD was further verified by calculating the kinetic data of enzymatic catalysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of GP73 with the ratiometric fluorescence aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: First, 20 μL of Cu-MOF-Apt solution was mixed with 20 μL of different concentrations of GP73 (25.00–600.00 ng/mL) and incubated at 35 °C for 5 h. Second, 20 μL of 30.00 μg/mL of BNCQDs, 20 μL of 100.00 mM of OPD solution, 20 μL of 200.00 mM of H2O2 solution, and 100 μL of 200.00 mM of HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 4.5) were added sequentially, and the incubation was carried out at 35 °C for 20 min with protection from light. The emission spectrum (EM) was observed at an excitation wavelength (EX) of 365 nm, and the fluorescence intensities at 445 nm and 560 nm were recorded. Third, a linear fitting equation was plotted with the concentration of GP73 as the independent variable and the fluorescence intensity ratio (I445/I560) as the dependent variable. Finally, the I445/I560 of the blank group was measured by fluorescence spectrophotometer without GP73 protein addition, then the standard deviation SD (Sblank) and LOD (3Sblank/slope) were calculated.

To achieve better GP73 detection, the concentrations of GP73Apt and OPD, the concentration ratio of OPD and H2O2, the pH of the buffer, and the incubation temperature and time were optimized for the ratio fluorescent aptasensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploring the specificity and repeatability of the ratiometric fluorescence aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: The specificity of the BNCQDs/Cu-MOF-Apt/DAP ratio fluorescent aptasensor was verified using common proteins in human serum (IgG, IgE, HSA, BSA) and clinical standard HCC marker (AFP) and potential HCC marker (GPC3) as the interfering substances. The concentration of GP73 was 500.00 ng/mL, while the concentration of interfering protein was 5.00 μg/mL. The concentration ratio of GP73 to the above interfering substances in the Mixture solution was 1:10.

Under optimal conditions, four fluorescence BNCQDs/Cu-MOF-Apt/DAP aptasensor were performed for the same concentration of GP73 (200.00 ng/mL). The four groups’ results of I445/I560 were compared, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was performed to verify the reproducibility of the BNCQDs/Cu-MOF-Apt/DAP ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GP73 in the human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the applicability of the developed ratiometric fluorescence aptasensor, clinical validation was performed through quantitative detection of GP73 in human serum specimens using the standard addition method, demonstrating the system's reliability in complex biological matrices. Human serum samples were acquired following the requirements of the Ethics Committee of the 924th Hospital of the Chinese People's Liberation Army Joint Logistic Support Force in Guilin, China (No. 2023-52). Nine kinds of human serum samples (1–9) were selected from healthy people (AFP <1.8 ng/mL, Clinical laboratory data). All the human serum samples were stored at −20 °C before use. Three different concentrations of GP73 solution were added to 10-fold diluted human serum samples (volume ratio, 1:1) to prepare the spiked samples for the recovery experiments. (Samples 1–3 contained 40.0 ng/mL GP73. Samples 4–6 contained 200.00 ng/mL GP73. Samples 7–9 contained 500.00 ng/mL GP73). The analysis procedure was similar to that described in Section 2.6.

Firstly, 20 μL of the spiked serum samples were added to 20 μL of Cu-MOF-Apt solution and incubated for 5 h at 35 °C. Secondly, 20 μL of 30.00 μg/mL BNCQDs, 20 μL of 100.00 mM OPD solution, 20 μL of 200.00 mM H2O2 solution, and 100 μL of 200.00 mM HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 4.5) were added, followed by incubation at 35 °C for 20 min with protection from light. Thirdly, the fluorescence intensities at 445 nm and 560 nm in the fluorescence emission spectrum at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm were recorded and the I445/I560 of each solution was obtained. Fourthly, the detected GP73 concentration in each spiked serum sample was calculated corresponding to the fitting equation. Finally, the recovery was calculated by dividing the spiked concentration by the added concentration. Three independent experiments were performed, and the data were shown as the mean ± SD (n = 3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection principle of the ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor for GP73
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 A illustrates the principle of the ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor. The system employs Cu-MOF-Apt as a bifunctional nanoprobe integrating catalytic activity and target recognition, with BNCQDs and DAP serving as dual fluorescence reporters. This unique configuration enables the establishment of a self-calibrating BNCQDs/Cu-MOF-Apt/DAP detection system. In the absence of GP73, the peroxidase-mimetic Cu-MOF-Apt facilitates H2O2 decomposition to generate hydroxyl radicals (•OH), which subsequently oxidize OPD to form DAP. The resultant DAP effectively quenches BNCQDs fluorescence through an inner filter effect (IFE), producing characteristic dual emission peaks at 445 nm (BNCQDs) and 560 nm (DAP) under 365 nm excitation. In the presence of target GP73, the strong affinity between Apt and GP73 and formation GP73-Apt complex in the system lead to the detachment of GP73Apt from the Cu-MOF-Apt surfaces, resulting in a decrease of catalytic activity, thereby reducing the production of DAP and changing in the ratiometric fluorescence signal. This attenuation progressively reduces DAP formation (I560 decrease) while restoring BNCQDs fluorescence (I445 increase), establishing a concentration-dependent I445/I560 ratio that allows precise GP73 quantification.

Herein, Cu-MOF can combine with amino-modified GP73Apt through π-π bond to form Cu-MOF-Apt [32]. When GP73 is introduced into the Cu-MOF-Apt/BNCQDs system, GP73Apt with high affinity could combine with GP73 to form a GP73-Apt complex. The formation of this complex will change the conformation of GP73Apt or shield the site of interaction between GP73Apt and Cu-MOF, which will reduce the binding force between Cu-MOF and GP73Apt, and then quickly separate Cu-MOF and GP73Apt, resulting in a decrease of catalytic activity, thereby reducing the production of DAP and changing the ratiometric fluorescence signal.

The feasibility of detecting GP73 was further explored by comparing the changes in the BNCQDs/Cu-MOF-Apt/DAP ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor with no GP73 and GP73 addition, and the results were shown in Fig. 1B. Curve a was the fluorescence spectrogram without adding GP73 while curve b was the fluorescence spectrogram with 1000 ng/mL of GP73 addition. It can be observed that the fluorescence intensity of BNCQDs (I445) increased, and the fluorescence intensity (I560) of DAP decreased with increasing protein concentration. In the order of GP73 concentration from low to high, the I445/I560 ratios were 0.8317 and 1.5757, respectively, indicating that the ratio would change with the GP73 concentration, which verified its feasibility.

Fig. 2 A demonstrated the basic optical properties of BNCQDs, which have an excitation wavelength (EX) of 380 nm and an emission wavelength (EM) of 470 nm. In addition, the states of BNCQDs with yellow color under natural light and blue color under UV light at 365 nm, which were seen in the inset of Fig. 2A. The UV–vis absorption spectrum showed a narrow peak at 200 nm and a broad peak at 320 nm, which is related to the doping of B and N elements [35].

The morphology of BNCQDs was characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM), and the results were shown in Fig. 2B. In the TEM image of 20 nm scale, BNCQDs is spherical, and the particle size of BNCQDs marked by red circles is about 3 nm. Furthermore, taking the TEM image of BNCQD on the scale of 5 nm (Fig. 2C), we can observe the lattice stripes, and the lattice spacing is 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (100) crystal plane of the graphite phase, and the spacing is reduced due to the doping of B.

From the full XPS spectrum of BNCQDs (Fig. 2D), the elemental contents of B1s, C1s, N1s, and O1s can be seen as 7.03 %, 57.86 %, 8.50 %, and 26.62 %, respectively, which indicated the successful doping of B and N elements. The two peaks of the B1s were located at 192.3 eV, 191.7 eV, and 188.8 eV, corresponding to B–O, B–C, and B–N. The two peaks of the N1s spectrum at 401.6 eV and 399.8 eV were N–H and C–N–C (Fig. 2E). The peaks of the C1s spectrum were located at 288.3 eV, 286.2 eV, 285.4 eV, 284.7 eV, and 283.8 eV corresponding to O–CO, C–N–C, C–O, C–C/CC, and C–B, respectively. The peaks of the O1s spectrum at 532.2 eV, 531.6 eV, and 532.0 eV corresponded to C–O, O–H, and B–O, respectively, which further proved the doping of B and N elements in BNCQDs.

Fig. 2F showed the FT-IR spectra of Cu-MOF. The peak at 3356.82 cm−1 arose from the vibrational stretching of the ligand and the –OH group in the water [36]. The stretching vibrations at 1576.55 cm−1 and 1552.28 cm−1 reflect the presence of carboxyl groups indicated by Cu-MOF, which arose from the symmetric and asymmetric vibrations of the C–O group in the carboxyl group. The pronounced peak at 1400.79 cm−1 and the small peak at 1439.98 cm−1 were attributed to the vibration of the phenyl ring backbone [37], and another peak at 751.32 cm−1 confirmed the substitution of copper in the phenyl group, which was attributed to the vibration of the Cu–O bond [29]. The FT-IR characterization of the Cu-MOF indicated the successful synthesis of the Cu-MOF as well as the carboxylate group at the surface of its presence.

From the SEM of Cu-MOF in Fig. 2G, it was observed that the Cu-MOF was in the form of flakes with a tetragonal shape, with a side length of about 2.00 μm. After EDS scanning based on Fig. 2H, it was found to contain Cu, O, C elements and a small amount of N element. The presence of N element was introduced by DMF. Fig. 2I showed the Mapping diagram of each element, and both Cu and O elements were concentrated on the scanned nanosheets, indicating the successful preparation of Cu-MOF.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of Cu-MOF powder was measured with a set scanning range of 5°–90° and a scanning rate of 10°/min, and the results are shown in Fig. 2J. Because the characteristic peaks of Cu-MOF are almost all within 50°, and there is no obvious peak from 50° to 90°, only the angular range of 5°–60° is shown in the figure. The characteristic peaks of Cu-MOF exist at 8.23°, 16.50°, 19.59°, 24.42°, 28.97°, and 42.06° for the prepared materials, which correspond to its (200), (420), (511), (222), (440), and (731) crystal planes, respectively [29].

From the full XPS spectrum of Cu-MOF (Fig. 2K), the elemental contents of C1s, N1s, and O1s, as well as the Cu2p spectrum of Cu-MOF, can be seen as 60.74 %, 3.24 %, 229.45 %, and 6.57 %, respectively. In the C1s spectrum, the peaks are located at energies corresponding to different bonds as follows: C–C (284.84 eV), C–N (285.63 eV), and CO (288.66 eV). For the N1s spectrum, the two peaks were for N–H (400.58 eV) and Cu–N (400.13 eV). In the O1s spectrum, the peaks corresponded to C–O (532.01 eV), O–H (533.59 eV), and Cu–O (531.67 eV). In the Cu2p spectrum, the peaks corresponded to Cu2+2p3/2 (933.44 eV), Cu+ (935.18 eV), Cu2+2p1/2 (953.08 eV), and Cu+ (954.18 eV) (Fig. 2L). All the above results indicated the successful preparation of Cu-MOF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of peroxidase-like properties of Cu-MOF and Cu-MOF-Apt
ce:section.ce:para: The peroxidase-like catalytic activity of Cu-MOF and Cu-MOF-Apt has been studied with the OPD + H2O2 system. The UV–vis absorption spectra of five groups of solutions were obtained according to the experimental procedure (Fig. 3 A). As seen from Fig. 3A, the absence of Cu-MOF (curve a, Fig. 3A) could not accelerate the decomposition of H2O2, resulting in no peak absorbance at 420 nm. Solutions b and c had no peaks due to the lack of an oxidizing agent H2O2, and a color-developing agent OPD, respectively (curves b, c, Fig. 3A). Solution d showed a significantly higher absorption spectrum than the other three groups (curve d, Fig. 3A), which verified the peroxidase-like activity of Cu-MOF. In addition, the absorbance of solution e at 420 nm was 0.34, and that of solution d at 420 nm was 0.23 (curve e, Fig. 3A), indicating that the binding of GP73Apt to Cu-MOF enhanced the peroxidase-like activity of Cu-MOF. Thus, the Cu-MOF-Apt possesses the highest peroxidase-like activity, followed by Cu-MOF, which exhibits good catalytic performance.

The enzymatic catalytic kinetics analysis under steady-state conditions for the Cu-MOF and Cu-MOF-Apt were investigated using either H2O2 or OPD as the substrate. The approach involved keeping the concentration of one substrate constant (H2O2 or OPD) while varying the concentration of the other. The kinetic parameters were derived from the absorption readings at 420 nm (Fig. 3B– D, Fig. 3F– H), which were recorded as a function of reaction time. These parameters were calculated employing the Michaelis-Menten Eq. (1)/V = Km/Vmax (1/[C] + 1 /Km) [30]. The inverse of the substrate concentration
1 /
[
O P D
]
and
1 /
[
H 2
O 2
]
and the inverse of the initial velocity of substrate concentration
1 / v
were calculated, respectively. The linear fits were performed and the results were shown in Fig. 3C– E, Fig. 3G–and Fig. 3I. The Mie constants (
K m
) and maximum reaction rates (
v max
) of both are detailed in Table 1 . The results showed that the exhibited catalytic profiles were really similar to that of the natural HRP enzyme [38]. Compared to the HRP, the Cu-MOF-Apt has a comparable affinity for OPD and a superior affinity for H2O2. The initial evidence suggests that the enhanced affinity for H2O2 could be the underlying cause for the improved peroxidase-like activity observed in Cu-MOF-Apt.

ERS/EPS characterization was carried out using paramagnetic resonance spectroscopy to observe the type of reactive oxygen species. Hydroxyl radicals (·OH) (Fig. 4 A), superoxide radicals (O2 ·-) (Fig. 4B), and singlet oxygen (1O2) (Fig. 4C) produced by the Cu-MOF-Apt solution were detected when H2O2 was just added or H2O2 was added for 15 min in the dark condition, respectively. Seen from Fig. 4A–C, none of the three transient radicals had characteristic peaks appearing without the addition of H2O2, indicating that Cu-MOF-Apt itself does not have the function of generating reactive oxygen species. After the addition of H2O2, it can be observed from Fig. 4A that there was a clear 1:2:2:1 appearance of ·OH with a large peak intensity, so ·OH is the main reactive oxygen component in the catalytic system of Cu-MOF-Apt and H2O2, which is also the main reason for being able to oxidize OPD. Meanwhile, the typical signals of smaller 1:1:1:1 O2 ·- (Fig. 4B) and the characteristic peaks of 1:1:1 1O2 (Fig. 4C), even though the intensities are lower, they don't appear in the characterization of MOF materials in the literature, so it is speculated that this may be the reason for the enhancement of peroxidase-like enzymes after the combination of Cu-MOF with GP73Apt. Thus, Cu-MOF-Apt exhibited enhanced peroxidase-like activity as well as a better affinity for H2O2 and catalyzed the generation of ·OH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of BNCQDs/Cu-MOF-Apt/DAP ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: In the UV–vis spectra of Cu-MOF (Fig. 4D), there is a distinct absorption peak at 200 nm, which may be caused by its organic ligand of terephthalic acid (TA). The absorption band at 240 nm may be caused by the transition from π to π∗ in Cu-MOF [31]. After Cu-MOF conjugated with GP73-specific aptamer to form Cu-MOF-Apt, there exhibited characteristic peaks at 200 nm, 240 nm, and small peaks from 260 nm to 280 nm, which may result from the formation of Cu-MOF-Apt. The morphologies of Cu-MOF and Cu-MOF-Apt were characterized by TEM (Fig. 4E and F). All the images have polyhedral structures. While Cu-MOF-Apt has thin and smooth nanosheets coating on polyhedral structures, which may be due to Apt covering on the surface of Cu-MOF.

Fig. 4G showed the absorption spectrum of DAP and the emission spectrum of BNCQDs. Seen from Fig. 4G, the absorption spectrum of DAP in the 400–500 nm range and the emission spectrum of BNCQDs overlap predominantly, which meets one of the conditions for the occurrence of inner filter effect (IFE) or resonance energy transfer (FRET). However, DAP is produced after OPD is oxidized and only exists in the solution. The physical distance between DAP and BNCQDs is not sufficient to cause FRET. Therefore, it is speculated that the decrease in the fluorescence intensity of BNCQDs after the addition of GP73 is mainly due to IFE.

On the other hand, we further analyzed the lifetime of BNCQDs and BNCQDs + DPA. As shown in Fig. 4H, the fluorescence lifetime of BNCQDs remained unchanged before and after adding DAP, indicating that the quenching of BNCQDs by DAP was not a mechanism that would change the fluorescence lifetime through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and so on. As we know, the IFE will not change the fluorescence lifetime of the fluorescent substance because it mainly reduces the intensity of excitation light reaching the fluorescent substance by absorbing photons rather than changing the fluorescence lifetime through direct energy or electron transfer with the fluorescent substance. Therefore, we speculate that the mechanism for DAP quenching the fluorescence of BNCQDs is attributed to IFE.

The zeta potentials of the BNCQDs/Cu-MOF-Apt/DAP ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor at each stage were analyzed in Fig. 4I. The potentials of the BNCQDs, Cu-MOF, Cu-MOF-Apt, Cu-MOF-Apt + GP73, and Cu-MOF-Apt+GP73+BNCQDs solutions were 0.4942, −5.7138, −12.3034, −9.0893, and −14.0289 mV, respectively. These indicated that the formed Cu-MOF-Apt was more stable than Cu-MOF, and the solution stability is the best after all the two fluorescent signals are imported, which shows the feasibility of this method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: The optimal experimental conditions were explored in order to obtain good performance for the ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor. The fluorescence intensity of the BNCQDs achieved a maximum under 380 nm excitation, while the DAP displayed the strongest fluorescence emission under 365 nm excitation. Thus, the ideal excitation wavelength was chosen as 365 nm to ensure the best visual effect of both BNCQDs and DAP. Many important conditions such as the concentrations of GP73Apt, the ratio of concentration of OPD and H2O2, the concentration of OPD, the pH value of HAc-NaAc buffer, the incubation temperature for OPD and the incubation time for OPD were optimized. The value of the ratio of I445/I560 was used to evaluate the pros and cons of the conditions.

The optimization results were shown in Fig. 5 . When the fluorescence intensity ratio (I445/I560) is at its minimum state, it means that the sensor is in a relatively “low-signal” background state at this time. On this basis, the signal changes will be easier to detect and observe, which can effectively improve the sensitivity of detection and the ability to distinguish the effect of GP73. Since the peroxidase-like activity of Cu-MOF-Apt would be enhanced after GP73Apt binding with Cu-MOF, resulting in a smaller I445/I560, when GP73Apt was 8 μM, the value of I445/I560 had the lowest ratio. Thus, 8 μM was chosen as the optimal concentration of GP73Apt (Fig. 5A).

Under the premise of fixing the concentration of BNCQD, the effect of the ratio of c (OPD):c (H2O2) and the concentration of OPD were optimized, as shown in Fig. 5B and C. The closer the peaks intensity of BNCQDs and DAP are to 1, the more conducive it is to the detection. Therefore, the optimal value of c (OPD): c (H2O2) was 1:2, and the optimal concentration of OPD was 100.00 mM. The influence of the pH value of HAc-NaAc buffer was exhibited in Fig. 5D. The value of I445/I560 was quite low when the pH was 4.5. With the increase of pH value, the value of I445/I560 increased. Thus, pH 4.5 was selected as the optimal pH of the HAc-NaAc buffer. The effect of the incubation temperature for OPD was optimized, as shown in Fig. 5E. With the incubation temperature for OPD increased, the fluorescence intensity of DAP at 560 nm increased. And the fluorescence intensity of DAP reached maximum at 35 °C, so the optimal incubation temperature was 35 °C. Fig. 5F showed the effect of the incubation time for OPD. Due to the extension of the incubation time, the increase of DAP leads to an excessively high fluorescence intensity of DAP, which is not conducive to the progress of the experiment. Therefore, the incubation time with the fluorescence intensity peak ratio of BNCQDs to DAP closest to 1 should be selected. Therefore, the optimal incubation time for OPD was 20 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimized conditions, the constructed ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor was used to achieve the detection of various concentrations of GP73. Fig. 6 A showed the fluorescence spectra at different concentrations of GP73. The fluorescence intensities of BNCQDs at 445 nm increased continuously, and the fluorescence intensities of DAP at 560 nm decreased as the GP73 concentration increased from 0 to 600 ng/mL. As the concentration of GP73 increased, more GP73Apt was taken away through the specific binding of GP73 with GP73Apt. The consequent decrease in the peroxidase-like activity of Cu-MOF led to the OPD to DAP. Thereby, the conversion of OPD to DAP was reduced. The quenching effect of DAP on BNCQDs was correspondingly reduced, which weakened the fluorescence intensity of DAP at 560 nm and enhanced the fluorescence intensity of BNCQDs at 445 nm.

Calculations showed that the GP73 concentrations of 25, 50, 100, 200, 300, 400, 500, and 600 ng/mL corresponded to I445/I560 of 0.7081, 0.7291, 0.7473, 0.8718, 0.9543, 0.9910, 1.0627, and 1.0914. A standard curve was then established between the GP73 concentration (in the range of 25–600 ng/mL) with the corresponding value of I445/I560, and the fitting curve was shown in Fig. 6B. As illustrated in Fig. 6B, the linear relationship between the I445/I560 and the GP73 concentration in the range of 25–600.0 ng/mL was determined to be Y = 0.0007X + 0.7021 (R2 = 0.976). Moreover, the LOD of this detection strategy was calculated to be 14.06 ng/mL by the triple signal-to-noise ratio (S/N = 3).

Furthermore, we compared some key parameters of our prepared fluorescence aptasensor for GP73 determination with other recently reported methods, and the results are displayed in Table 2 . As seen from Table 2, the prepared aptasensor showed a wide linear range of 25.00–600.00 ng/mL with the advantages of high sensitivity and selectivity. The fluorescence method has a more convenient and rapid detection process than other methods. Furthermore, the BNCQDs/Cu-MOF-Apt/DAP ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor has a better detection range than the monochromatic fluorescent method, which is more in line with the clinical requirements for the detection of GP73 concentration in human serum, providing a new way of constructing fast fluorescence aptamer sensing methods in the field of biomarker analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity and reproducibility of the ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the specificity of the prepared aptasensors, a series of interferents (AFP, GPC3, IgG, IgE, BSA, and HSA) with a concentration of 5000.0 ng/mL and GP73 concentration of 500.0 ng/mL were prepared and detected by the constructed aptasensor. The results of the specificity examination of the ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor were shown in Fig. 6C. The I445/I560 value of GP73, Mixture, AFP, GPC3, IgG, IgE, BSA, and HSA were 1.0627, 0.9867, 0.6369, 0.6489, 0.6549, 0.6667, 0.6899, and 0.6683, respectively. The small difference between the GP73 protein and the others is that the fitted curves do not change much, as illustrated by Blank's data. The reason that the I445/I560 of the mixed solution is lower than GP73 may be that some substances in the serum have some effect on the fluorescence properties of the quantum dots. All the results indicated that the ratiometric fluorescence aptasensor had better specificity and selectivity for GP73 detection.

The reproducibility of the GP73 ratiometric fluorescence aptasensor was also explored. Four fluorescence BNCQDs/Cu-MOF-Apt/DAP aptasensors were performed for the same concentration of GP73 (200.00 ng/mL). The results of the reproducibility of the ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor were shown in Fig. 6D. The I445/I560 values of the four samples were 0.8776, 0.7840, 0.84974, and 0.7951, respectively. And the RSD value was 4.40 %, which verified good reproducibility of the ratiometric fluorescence aptasensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GP73 in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: As a new tumor biomarker, the determination of GP73 serum level has no standard clinical reference method. At present, most related studies have used a standard addition recovery experiment to detect GP73 in actual serum [40,42]. Therefore, in this study, GP73 in serum samples was detected by a standard addition recovery experiment to evaluate the ratiometric fluorescent aptamer sensor's practicability and clinical application. Three different concentrations of GP73 solution were added to 10-fold diluted human serum samples (volume ratio, 1:1) to prepare the spiked samples for the recovery experiments. (Samples 1–3 contained 40.0 ng/mL GP73. Samples 4–6 contained 200.00 ng/mL GP73. Samples 7–9 contained 500.00 ng/mL GP73). The analysis procedure was described in Section 2.10 in detail, and the results were shown in Table 3 . The results in Table 3 showed that the relative standard deviations (RSD) of this fluorescent aptasensor for the detection of GP73 concentration in human serum were 0.28 %–0.98 %, and the recovery rates were 99.75 %–107.49 % in the assay range. The above data indicated that the ratiometric fluorescent aptasensor was sufficiently accurate and effective for use with real serum samples.

Herein, we successfully synthesized BNCQDs with excellent fluorescence properties and Cu-MOF with peroxidase activity by the hydrothermal method. The novel ratiometric sensing platform ingeniously integrated BNCQDs (λem = 445 nm) as reference fluorophores with DAP (λem = 560 nm) as responsive indicators, while Cu-MOF-Apt served dual functions as catalytic amplifiers and molecular recognition elements. The sensing paradigm relies on target-induced structural reorganization. In the absence of GP73, the peroxidase-mimetic Cu-MOF-Apt facilitates H2O2 decomposition to generate •OH, which subsequently oxidizes OPD to form DAP. The resultant DAP effectively quenches BNCQDs' fluorescence through an inner filter effect IFE. In the presence of target GP73, the strong affinity between Apt and GP73 and the formation GP73-Apt complex in the system lead to the detachment of GP73Apt from the Cu-MOF-Apt surfaces, resulting in a decrease of catalytic activity, thereby reducing the production of DAP. This attenuation progressively reduces DAP formation (I560 decrease) while restoring BNCQDs fluorescence (I445 increase), establishing a concentration-dependent I445/I560 ratio that allows precise GP73 quantification. Under the best experimental conditions, the two-color fluorescent probe showed a good linear relationship with the detection range of 25.00–600.00 ng/mL and the lowest detection limit of 14.06 ng/mL. In practical application, we used the standard addition recovery to determine the level of GP73 in actual serum, and the RSD was between 0.28 % and 0.98 %, and the recovery rates was between 99.75 % and 107.49 %, which showed high detection accuracy and reliability. This aptasensor can detect GP73 linearly in a wide concentration range, which provides a powerful tool for disease diagnosis and disease monitoring. By introducing the double fluorescence signal, the error caused by the interference of a single signal by environmental factors is effectively reduced, and the accuracy and stability of detection are greatly improved. In addition, this study also laid a foundation for the subsequent visualization or portable detection of GP73 through the change of fluorescence color, which is expected to further expand the application of this technology in the field of on-site rapid detection and inject new vitality into the development of POCT technology.
